title: Interpreting Alignment Scores
text: Alignment Score (SaS_a) measures degree consensus researchers community partners across key aspects project. answers fundamental question: “see partnership way?” High alignment doesn’t just mean everyone agrees, means researchers partners share common understanding work carried . Misalignment, conversely, reveals assumptions, communication gaps, power imbalances need attention.

title: Interpreting Alignment Scores
text: Priority Mapping process informed CEnTR*IMPACT development revealed community-engaged scholars value shared decision-making, prioritizing community needs, relationship building traditional indicators like contact hours participant counts. Alignment captures values quantitatively. Research community-based participatory research (CBPR) consistently shows alignment goals, values, processes predicts project success, sustainability, equitable outcomes (Wallerstein & Duran, 2010). researchers partners view partnership differently, projects risk producing outputs miss community needs, eroding trust, failing build lasting capacity.

title: Interpreting Alignment Scores
text: Alignment measured across eight dimensions emerged CBPR framework Priority Mapping process: Goals: purposes aims project Values: ideals principles guide work Roles: Responsibilities contributions party Resources: assets, funding, capabilities managed shared Activities: design facilitation events interventions Empowerment: community culture, knowledge, language honored Outputs: types products created (workshops, articles, policy documents) Outcomes: Short-term long-term changes expected achieved factor rated researchers community partners 0 1 scale, 1 represents complete alignment 0 represents alignment.

title: Interpreting Alignment Scores
text: overall Alignment Score (SaS_a) calculated using Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC), measures well researchers’ ratings resemble community partners’ ratings across eight factors. ICC provides value 0 1 representing agreement.

title: Interpreting Alignment Scores

what: Pattern Analysis: What to Look For
title: Interpreting Alignment Scores
text: 1. Strong Mutual Alignment (scores close together) groups rate factor similarly (within ~0.10), indicates genuine shared understanding. Values showing close alignment means strong foundation shared principles build upon. Note Resources shows slightly larger gap (0.11) 0.10, included “strong alignment” category scores relatively high (>0.65), indicating overall positive perception despite gap. 2. Researcher-Heavy Perception (researchers rate much higher) Large gaps researchers rate higher (>0.20 difference) suggest researchers perceive strength clarity partners don’t experience. pattern often indicates: Resources: Researchers feel well-equipped, partners may know ’s available access resources Outputs: Researchers satisfied deliverables, partners find less useful relevant Roles: Researchers see clear definitions, partners experience ambiguity feel constrained 3. Partner-Leading Perception (partners rate much higher) partners rate factors higher researchers, may: - Feel positively agency impact researchers realize - See potential change researchers cautious claiming - Experience benefits researchers don’t fully recognize 4. Mutual Low Scores (groups rate low) groups rate factor low (< 0.50), signals: - dimension genuinely needs development - Shared recognition area requiring attention - Potential opportunity collaborative improvement 5. Mutual High Scores (groups rate high) groups rate factor high (> 0.75), indicates: - genuine strength maintain leverage - Successful practices worth documenting replicating - Foundation addressing areas

title: Interpreting Alignment Scores
text: Mistake 1: “Higher scores always better” necessarily. Outcomes score 0.90 ’re 3 months project suggests inflated expectations rather genuine alignment. Context matters. Mistake 2: “Low alignment means failure” Low alignment means ’ve identified areas needing attention. ’s diagnostic tool, judgment. Many successful partnerships started low alignment improved intentional dialogue. Mistake 3: “aim perfect alignment” healthy tension productive. Researchers partners bring different expertise perspectives. goal sufficient alignment work effectively, identical viewpoints. Mistake 4: “Partners rated us low ’re dissatisfied” always. Lower partner ratings Resources might mean don’t know ’s available. Lower ratings Empowerment might reflect realistic assessment structural constraints. Explore assuming. Mistake 5: “High alignment Values enough” Values alignment necessary insufficient. need operational alignment (Resources, Roles, Activities) translate shared principles effective action.

title: Interpreting Cascade Effects Scores
text: Cascade Effects Score (Sc) measures potential information power spread project’s social network across three degrees separation. ’s based fundamental insight community-engaged research: direct participants aren’t whole story. train community health workers (1st degree), teach neighbors (2nd degree), influence extended networks (3rd degree). work parent leaders, impact ripples schools, neighborhoods, social groups. Cascade Effects quantifies ripple. Unlike traditional evaluation counts direct participants, cascade analysis recognizes community work creates waves influence. project reaching 50 people directly catalyzing three degrees cascade achieves fundamentally different impact one reaching 200 ripple effect.

title: Interpreting Cascade Effects Scores
text: famous “six degrees separation” theory suggests global interconnectedness, research shows direct personal influence extends three degrees (Christakis & Fowler, 2009). Beyond three degrees, information becomes diluted influence weakens substantially. three degrees community-engaged research: 1st Degree: Core participants directly involved (faculty, staff, students, primary community partners) 2nd Degree: Immediate networks core participants—people ’re likely share project experiences meaningfully 3rd Degree: Individuals within broader community 2nd degree participants likely influence shared knowledge experiences

what: How Cascade Differs from Ripple Mapping
title: Interpreting Cascade Effects Scores
text: ’re familiar ripple effects mapping (Chazdon et al., 2017), Cascade Effects takes related distinct approach: valuable—ripple mapping captures lived experiences impact, cascade analysis reveals structural capacity information/power distribution.

title: Interpreting Cascade Effects Scores
text: Cascade Effects examines four properties together reveal well information power can spread:

title: Interpreting Cascade Effects Scores
text: measures: People connect different groups wouldn’t otherwise interact much Social network metrics: Structural holes (constraint) + Degree centrality inter-layer graph matters: Without bridge builders, information stays siloed within specific communities sectors. Bridges enable knowledge travel different neighborhoods, organizations, demographic groups, social circles. High bridging looks like: - community health worker participates Latinx parent group neighborhood association - researcher sits university community foundation boards - youth leader active high school mosque Low bridging looks like: - Core team members come organization - Participant networks don’t overlap - Information doesn’t cross demographic geographic boundaries

title: Interpreting Cascade Effects Scores
text: measures: People serve key gateways bottlenecks information Social network metrics: PageRank (local flow) + Harmonic centrality (global transmission) inter-layer graph matters: Channelers control whether quickly information spreads. can either accelerate sharing become bottlenecks restrict flow. healthy networks, channeling distributed; problematic ones, ’s concentrated. High channeling looks like: - Multiple pathways information flow - Distributed communication responsibility - Information reaches people various routes Low channeling looks like: - Single person acts information gatekeeper - one two people stop participating, information flow collapses - Communication bottlenecks slow knowledge sharing

title: Interpreting Cascade Effects Scores
text: measures: People connect influential individuals within communities, creating cohesive networks Social network metrics: Community centrality + Eigenvector centrality matters: Knitters create resilient social structures linking leaders, elders, influential community members. influential people connected , communities can sustain spread information even without external facilitation. High knitting looks like: - Community elders know work - Organizational leaders collaborate regularly - Influential members form collective leadership structures Low knitting looks like: - Influential people isolated - Leaders don’t know leaders - Fragmented leadership unable coordinate collective action

title: Interpreting Cascade Effects Scores
text: radial visualization (inspired W.E.B. DuBois’ pioneering data visualizations) shows: Rings: represents degree (1°, 2°, 3° center outward) Spokes: Four spokes per ring represent four properties Spoke length: Longer = higher score property degree Visual patterns notice: Shrinking pattern: Spokes get shorter moving outward (typical cascade decay) Uniform shrinkage: properties decline together (systemic limitation) Specific short spokes: Individual properties notably weaker (targeted intervention opportunity) Abrupt drops: Sharp decline degrees (structural barriers) example, can see: - Strong, relatively equal spokes 1° (solid core) - Moderate, slightly uneven spokes 2° (bridging/channeling slightly weaker) - Short, weak spokes 3° (especially knitting)

title: Interpreting Cascade Effects Scores
text: Mistake 1: “Bigger networks always better” necessarily. network 100 poorly connected people less cascade potential 30 well-connected people. Quality connections matters quantity. Mistake 2: “Low cascade scores mean project failure” Low scores early stages expected. Cascade develops time relationships deepen strategies take effect. Mistake 3: “focus expanding 3°” Weak 3° often indicates weak 2°. Strengthen 2° first—trying leapfrog rarely works. Mistake 4: “Perfect balance across degrees goal” natural decline expected—influence diminishes distance. Moderate balance (Sc 0.60-0.75) strong absolute scores excellent. Mistake 5: “Cascade scores measure actual impact” Cascade scores measure potential based network structure. predict capacity spread, guarantee . Actual impact requires structural capacity quality content/relationships spread.

title: Interpreting Project Dynamics Scores
text: Project Dynamics Score (Sd) provides insight project carried across five key domains derived Community-Based Participatory Research (CBPR) framework. answers question: “balanced attention across essential dimensions community-engaged work?” Unlike metrics measure “better,” Project Dynamics measures balance. project excels research design neglects partnership processes, emphasizes outputs ignoring capacity-building, demonstrates poor dynamics even areas strong. Transformative community-engaged research requires attention across domains.

title: Interpreting Project Dynamics Scores
text: Priority Mapping process informed CEnTR*IMPACT revealed community-engaged scholars value shared decision-making, purposeful approaches, co-constructed infrastructure alongside research rigor. Simply producing many outputs serving many people isn’t enough processes extractive community priorities ignored. Balance Project Dynamics indicates: - Holistic rather narrow approach - Sustainable rather transactional relationships - Mutual transformation rather one-sided benefit - Integration across research, practice, learning Projects poor balance often: - Overemphasize one domain expense others - Neglect foundation-building (Contexts, Partnerships) - Focus Outcomes without nurturing processes - Separate research relationship-building

title: Interpreting Project Dynamics Scores
text: overall Project Dynamics Score (Sd) uses complement Gini coefficient (Gc) applied domain scores. Gini coefficient measures inequality; complement measures balance. Within domain: 1. Descriptors selected ranked relevance/importance 2. Descriptor values calculated using assigned weights × ranking weights 3. Dimension scores computed weighted means 4. Domain scores derived geometric means dimension scores 5. Overall Sd calculated balance across domain scores multi-level calculation ensures : - relevant descriptors contribute scores - important descriptors carry weight - Balance measured across domains - ’re penalized incomplete descriptor selection

title: Interpreting Project Dynamics Scores
text: Critical insight: High balance low absolute domain scores indicates even shallow development. Moderate balance high absolute scores may indicate intentional, stage-appropriate focus. Always examine balance absolute levels.

title: Interpreting Project Dynamics Scores
text: Mistake 1: “Perfect balance means perfect project” High balance (Sd > 0.90) low absolute scores (domains < 0.40) indicates balanced mediocrity, excellence. Focus balance strength. Mistake 2: “Low balance means failure” Strategic imbalance may appropriate. Early projects naturally emphasize Contexts/Partnerships Outcomes. Phase-appropriate imbalance isn’t problematic. Mistake 3: “Higher domain scores always better” Prematurely high Outcomes (0.80 Month 3) signals inflated expectations rather genuine achievement. Context matters. Mistake 4: “work domains equally” Focus 1-2 priority domains time. Trying improve everything simultaneously overwhelms teams. Mistake 5: “score story” Dynamics scores entry points conversation, conclusions. qualitative “” behind scores matters numbers alone.

title: Interpreting Project Dynamics Scores
text: Use rose diagram: Visual makes patterns immediately clear Frame developmentally: “’re building strong foundation across areas. move forward, ’ll see petals grow.” Invite interpretation: “notice visualization? match experience?” Co-plan actions: “area focus strengthening next?”

text: centrimpact R package provides comprehensive toolkit analyzing visualizing community engaged research metrics highlighted CEnTR*IMPACT framework (Price, 2024). offers specialized functions researcher-partner alignment, project dynamics, cascade effects, creating publication-ready visualizations. Based CENTR*IMPACT framework, package quantifies following critical concepts community engaged research: Alignment: shared vision researchers partners (SaS_a). Cascade Effects: ripple effect work direct participants third degree separation (ScS_c). Dynamics: Ways project carried modeled CBPR framework (SdS_d). Indicators: “Traditional” markers academic productivity, individuals served, number outputs, etc.

title: Run the Cascade Analysis Pipeline — analyze_cascade
text: Routing Logic: expected edge count estimated analytically survey parameters without constructing network. falls within max_edges, single full calculate_cascade(build_network()) call made. Otherwise, five "people" parameters scaled proportionally resulting network approximately target_nodes total nodes, n_runs independent runs averaged. Scaling Method: scale factor \(s\) found solving cubic node-count equation $$n_1 s + n_2 s^2 + n_3 s^3 = \text{target\_nodes}$$ numerically via uniroot(), \(n_1\), \(n_2\), \(n_3\) unscaled node counts per layer. preserves L1:L2:L3 ratio avoids floor-collapse problem occurs fixed multiplier small counts round zero. Floors pmax(2, ...) applied L1 type counts (clique remains meaningful) pmax(1, ...) counts. average runs? probabilistic edges (L2-L2, L3-L3, back-edges) introduce stochastic variance. single scaled run can produce misleading scores scaled network small. Averaging n_runs independent realizations scaled parameters yields stable estimates; summary_sd quantifies remaining run--run variance.

title: Analyze Project Dynamics — analyze_dynamics
text: CEnTR*IMPACT framework uses analysis quantify "Developmental Balance." higher Balance Score indicates project maintaining healthy equilibrium across various dimensions, rather -indexing just one area expense others. Scoring Process: Dimension Scoring: descriptor, weight (\(w_j\)) multiplied Smith's salience index (\(s_j\)). dimension score geometric mean across descriptors. $$s_{\text{dimension}} = \left(\prod_{j=1}^{n} (w_j \times s_j)\right)^{1/n}$$ Domain Scoring: Aggregates dimension scores within domain using geometric mean across dimensions domain. $$s_{\text{domain}} = \left(\prod_{=1}^{m} s_{\text{dimension},}\right)^{1/m}$$ Dynamics Scoring: Calculated based inequality domain scores. $$S_{d} = 1 - \operatorname{Gini}(\{s_{\text{domain},k}\})$$ Dynamics Score Interpretation: following rule thumb (Haddad et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2020) used interpret Dynamics Score (\(S_d\)): \(S_d < 0.5\): Low Balance \(0.50 \le S_d < 0.59\): Low Balance \(0.60 \le S_d < 0.69\): Moderate Balance \(0.70 \le S_d \le 0.79\): High Balance \(S_d \ge 0.80\): High Balance Handling Missing Data: function permissive partial datasets. domain included input contains NA values weights salience, retained output. However, sufficient data missing prevent calculation, score NaN.

title: Calculate Cascade Metrics From a Network Edge List — calculate_cascade
text: Theoretical Foundation: Unlike general connectivity metrics, method resembles work Long, Cunningham, Braithwaite (2013), examining participants' roles based structure network formed research. assesses influence ripples outward core team (Layer 1) broader community (Layer 3+). Operational Definitions: function maps Social Network Analysis (SNA) metrics four key influence roles: 1. Knitting (Cohesion & Bonding): Measures well network strengthens internal bonds within specific group. Metrics: Community detection (Walktrap) + Eigenvector Centrality. Interpretation: High scores indicate tight-knit, resilient core. 2. Bridging (Connection & Spanning): Measures ability connect otherwise disconnected groups (filling "structural holes"). Metrics: Structural Holes (Constraint) + Degree Centrality inter-layer graph. Interpretation: High scores indicate key "brokers" connecting silos. 3. Channeling (Flow & Transmission): Measures efficiency information flow resource distribution. Metrics: PageRank (local) + Harmonic Centrality (global) inter-layer graph. Interpretation: High scores indicate effective pipelines moving resources. 4. Reaching (Access & Inclusion): Measures extent network's periphery accessibility. Metrics: Clustering Coefficient + Communicability (matrix exponential) inter-layer graph. Interpretation: High scores indicate inclusive network reduced barriers. Scoring Process: Layer (Degree) Scoring: network layer (degree separation), influence calculated combining local cohesion (Knitting + Bridging), global flow (Channeling), peripheral access (Reaching): $$s_{\text{layer}} = \gamma(\alpha L + \beta G) + \lambda T$$ \(L\) represents combined Knitting Bridging scores, \(G\) represents Channeling score, \(T\) represents Reaching score, weights : \(\alpha = 0.4\) (local cohesion weight) \(\beta = 0.3\) (global flow weight) \(\lambda = 0.3\) (peripheral access weight) \(\gamma\) varies layer: 0.9 (Layer 1), 0.5 (Layer 2), 0.45 (Layer 3) Cascade Balance Score: Calculated based equality layer scores. $$S_{c} = 1 - \operatorname{Gini}(\{s_{\text{layer},k}\})$$ \(\{s_{\text{layer},k}\}\) represents set layer influence scores. Cascade Balance Interpretation: \(S_c < 0.50\): Low Balance (Core-dominated) \(0.50 \le S_c < 0.59\): Low Balance \(0.60 \le S_c < 0.69\): Moderate Balance \(0.70 \le S_c \le 0.79\): High Balance \(S_c \ge 0.80\): High Balance (Equitable distribution)

title: Load and Clean CEnTR*IMPACT Survey Data — load_survey_data
text: Survey types: survey = "main" reads primary CEnTR*IMPACT survey containing indicators, project dynamics, cascade network parameters. function detects export format automatically: Google Forms: identified presence Timestamp column. Verbose question-text headers translated compact variable names, dynamics_selection (absent form) inserted NA, cascade probability fields rescaled 1–10 integer scale \([0, 1]\). Qualtrics: identified presence Qualtrics system columns (StartDate, ResponseId, etc.). System meta columns, descriptor rows, _selection columns removed. " Applicability" suffix stripped rating values "Applicabie" typo corrected. Returns named list three analysis-ready data frames: indicators, dynamics, cascade. survey = "alignment" reads alignment survey containing researcher community partner ratings across eight partnership factors. Qualtrics system columns descriptor rows removed present. Returns single long-format data frame ready analyze_alignment(). Multiple rows: survey types formats, one data row present cleaning, last row kept. handles Qualtrics exports include preview test responses final submission.

title: Visualize Researcher-Partner Alignment (Abacus Plot) — visualize_abacus
text: Methodology: Inspired "Degree Collaboration Tool" (Doberneck & Dann, 2019), visualization maps multiple dimensions project onto horizontal tracks (0 1). adapted CEnTR*IMPACT framework (Price, 2024) show consensus spread survey ratings. Visual Elements: Rails: Horizontal gray bars representing full continuum possible agreement (0 1). Solid Beads: median rating group (Researchers vs. Partners). Transparent Beads: minimum maximum ratings, showing spread disagreement within group. Overall Square: geometric mean (consensus) rating. format particularly useful identifying "outliers" (individuals rated extremely high low) relative group's median.

title: Visualize Cascade (Ripple) Effects — visualize_cascade
text: Historical Context: often called "racetrack plot" modern plotting libraries, visualization style pays homage "City Rural Population 1890" spiral charts created W.E.B. Du Bois 1900 Paris Exposition. Du Bois effectively used wrapped bars display magnitude within confined space, allowing immediate visual comparison lengths (impact) without distortions standard pie charts. Interpretation: plot maps "Ripple Effect" project: Inner Ring (1st Degree: Immediate impact (Direct participants). Middle Ring (2nd Degree): Intermediate impact (Partners partners, local community). Outer Ring (3rd Degree): Distant impact (Policy changes, broader field adoption). length arc represents score (0 1). "full" track indicates maximum impact level.

title: Visualize Dynamics (Rose Diagram) — visualize_dynamics
text: Historical Context: visualization adapted "Diagram Causes Mortality Army East" Florence Nightingale (1858). Just diagram highlighted disproportionate causes death, chart highlights disproportionate strengths weaknesses project infrastructure. Visual Metaphor: chart separates data two distinct layers show "Forest" (Domain) "Trees" (Dimensions): Petals (Background Wedges): Represent aggregated Domain score. form background "fan." Domain strong, petal reaches outer edge (1.0). weak, shrinks toward center. Stamen (Foreground Lollipops): Represent specific Dimension values. radiating lines allow see specific dimension (e.g., "Trust") lagging behind parent Domain (e.g., "Partnerships"). Interpretation: well-balanced project appear full, nearly circular bloom. Gaps "wilted" sectors indicate areas project infrastructure (Context, Partnerships, Research, Learning, Outcomes) require attention.
